response:
The quadratic equation for the average batting score is \( S = 50 + 5n - 0.2n^2 \).
This parabola opens downward because the coefficient of \( n^2 \) is negative.
The maximum value occurs at the vertex of the parabola.
The formula for the vertex coordinate \( n \) is \( -b / 2a \).
Here \( b \) equals 5 and \( a \) equals -0.2.
Substituting these values gives \( n = -5 / (2 \times -0.2) \).
The calculation simplifies to \( n = -5 / -0.4 \).
The result is exactly 12.5 practice sessions per month.
Since practice sessions are usually whole numbers we check 12 and 13.
For \( n = 12 \) the score is \( 50 + 60 - 28.8 \) which equals 81.2.
For \( n = 13 \) the score is \( 50 + 65 - 33.8 \) which equals 81.2.
Both integer values yield the same maximum average batting score of 81.2.
The theoretical maximum using the exact vertex is 81.25.
We will use the exact vertex value for the probability calculation.
The win probability formula is \( P = 1 / (1 + e^{-(0.1S - 3)}) \).
First we calculate the score when \( n \) equals 5.
The score becomes \( 50 + 25 - 5 \) which equals 70.
The exponent term is \( 0.1 \times 70 - 3 \) which equals 4.
The probability for 5 sessions is \( 1 / (1 + e^{-4}) \).
Next we calculate the score when \( n \) equals 10.
The score becomes \( 50 + 50 - 20 \) which equals 80.
The exponent term is \( 0.1 \times 80 - 3 \) which equals 5.
The probability for 10 sessions is \( 1 / (1 + e^{-5}) \).
The difference in probability is the value for 10 minus the value
